sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content:
Surgery is the primary curative treatment for penile cancer, aiming to achieve negative margins while preserving penile function when feasible. It includes organ-sparing approaches for early-stage disease and more extensive procedures for advanced tumors.

Principles:
- Organ-sparing techniques are preferred for PeIN, Ta, and select T1a lesions to preserve quality of life and sexual function.
- Partial penectomy is standard for high-grade tumors (≥pT1b or ≥T2) if a functional stump can be preserved with negative margins.
- Total penectomy is necessary when partial penectomy cannot achieve negative margins or for extensive invasion.
- Intraoperative frozen sections are recommended to ensure negative surgical margins.
- Surgical management of lymph nodes includes inguinal lymph node dissection (ILND) and pelvic lymph node dissection (PLND) based on risk stratification and nodal status.

Resectability Criteria:
Resectability is based on tumor stage, location, and invasion into adjacent structures. For primary tumors, invasion into corpora cavernosum may require penectomy. For lymph nodes, resectability is determined by size (<4 cm mobile nodes are resectable), fixation (fixed nodes may be borderline resectable), and presence of distant metastases. Bulky inguinal nodes (≥4 cm or fixed) may require neoadjuvant chemotherapy to downstage for surgery [PN-5, PN-7].

Pre-operative Optimization:
Pre-operative optimization includes assessment of performance status, management of comorbidities, and nutritional support. For patients with bulky nodal disease, neoadjuvant TIP chemotherapy is preferred to downstage disease and improve surgical outcomes. Infection control is important, especially for overlying skin infections [PN-D, MS-13].

cards.name: Wide local excision
cards.detail: Approach: Excision of the penile lesion with wide negative margins; may include skin grafting. Indications: PeIN, Ta, T1a lesions confined to skin with little invasion. Key complications: Local recurrence (up to 50% at 2 years if margins inadequate), wound infection, skin graft failure. Operative mortality: Low; not specified in text.

cards.name: Glansectomy
cards.detail: Approach: Removal of the glans penis; may be followed by split- or full-thickness skin graft for neoglans reconstruction. Indications: Distal tumors on glans or prepuce (PeIN, Ta, T1a); not recommended for pT1a unless needed for negative margins. Key complications: Local recurrence (2.6%–16.7%), urinary complications, erectile dysfunction. Operative mortality: Low; not specified in text.

cards.name: Partial penectomy
cards.detail: Approach: Removal of the distal penis with preservation of a functional stump; intraoperative frozen sections for margins. Indications: High-grade primary tumors (≥pT1b or ≥T2) where functional stump can be preserved. Key complications: Positive margins requiring re-resection, urinary fistula, sexual dysfunction. Operative mortality: Low; not specified in text.

cards.name: Total penectomy
cards.detail: Approach: Complete removal of the penis; may include urethrostomy. Indications: When partial penectomy cannot achieve negative margins or for T4 tumors invading adjacent structures. Key complications: Urinary diversion complications, psychological impact, sexual dysfunction. Operative mortality: Low; not specified in text.

cards.name: Inguinal lymph node dissection (ILND)
cards.detail: Approach: Standard or modified template; standard includes Daseler’s quadrilateral area. Indications: Staging or treatment of inguinal lymph node metastases; for intermediate/high-risk non-palpable nodes or palpable nodes. Key complications: Lymphedema, wound infection, skin necrosis, seroma. Operative mortality: Low; not specified in text.

cards.name: Pelvic lymph node dissection (PLND)
cards.detail: Approach: Resection of external iliac, internal iliac, and obturator lymph nodes; unilateral or bilateral. Indications: ≥2 positive inguinal nodes on ipsilateral ILND or extranodal extension; ≥4 positive inguinal nodes (bilateral PLND). Key complications: Lymphedema, vascular injury, infection. Operative mortality: Low; not specified in text.
